data.title: Ride The Fat Dividend Train, +10% Yield: Capital Southwest
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Being the youngest in my family, I always had the desire to measure up to or beat my older brothers. We were all skilled in very different ways. My one brother creates amazing art, something none of us could best. Eventually, we all found our natural rhythm and focused on our specific skill sets; the childish games of beating one another as a measure of success fell by the wayside.

In the market, often our competitiveness can get the best of us as well. Many investors feel they're only winning if they "beat the market" (SP500), as if that total return is arbitrarily the only way to measure success. However, the success I need and desire financially may not be the best way for you to meet your individual goals. A measuring stick is only useful if it adequately helps you measure movement toward your personal goals. Don't get caught up in childish competition and lose focus on what you're working towards.

This is a big reason why I am a massive advocate for actionable goals when it relates to your portfolio. Goals you can measure, have a time limit, and aren't vague or open-ended, like "I want to beat the market" or "I want to have $1,000,000". Pick something that you can measure that works and corresponds with your goals

Today, I want to dive into a company's earnings that many of my frequent readers will recognize as a top-notch income generator. It's been a key player in helping me achieve my annual income goals.

Let's dive in!

A Focus On Growing Its Portfolio

Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC), yielding 10.1%, is a business development company, BDC, that lends to middle-market companies within the United States. Many BDCs focus on a niche section or approach to this mostly private realm of the market. Unlike many peers, CSWC focuses on the lower middle market companies - firms with $5 million to $50 million in annual revenue.

CSWC is giving us a little extra income this quarter, announcing the regular dividends as unchanged at $0.58, and announcing a supplemental dividend of $0.06. The supplement dividend last quarter was $0.05.

It is important to keep a perspective on what supplemental dividends are. When a company is differentiating between a "regular" and a "supplemental" dividend, management is telling you something. CSWC has paid a supplemental dividend for 10 quarters in a row. After such a long period, it is easy to get used to the supplement and just assume that it will continue indefinitely. It won't. That's why it is labeled as a "supplement". CSWC's business model is to borrow at fixed rates and lend at floating rates. As a result, CSWC has enjoyed a boost to earnings which is primarily due to high interest rates and the slow pace of the cutting cycle so far.

Management separates the dividend into two parts. The "regular" dividend is a dividend that we can expect to continue absent something unfavorable happening. It is an amount that management believes will be sustainable in a wide variety of interest rate conditions. The "supplement" is a distribution that relies on the current environment of above-average earnings.

CSWC has a great track record of producing excess earnings and paying supplements and special dividends. However, the supplement can disappear without anything going "wrong" with the company. Source

The dividend yield in the Model Portfolio includes the $0.06 supplement for this quarter, but it does not assume any supplement will be announced in future quarters.

Take supplements for what they are: CSWC is enjoying especially favorable conditions for their business and is sharing the excess with investors.

For the 3rd quarter (their FY ends March 31st), CSWC's earnings were very stable. CSWC's pre-tax NII came in at $0.64, exactly the same as last quarter. NAV at $16.59 unchanged from last quarter.

Non-accruals were at 2.7% of the portfolio, down from 3.5% last quarter. CSWC ranks their investments in four categories, with "1" being the strongest, suggesting companies that are outperforming underwriting expectations, and "4" being the weakest investments where management anticipates a loss is probable.

CSWC saw some material improvement in credit quality, with 9 loans upgraded and 3 loans downgraded:

CSWC has been taking advantage of its share price trading at a premium to NAV to issue equity. After a relatively flat year, CSWC increased the size of its portfolio last quarter to $1.78 billion, up from $1.6 billion the prior quarter.

In short, a rather flat quarter from the headline numbers, but CSWC saw improvements in non-accruals, credit quality and had more aggressive growth that will benefit future quarters. This is the first meaningful portfolio growth we have seen in about a year. Growing the portfolio will provide room for earnings growth that is sustainable even as the interest rate environment changes.

Conclusion

Among the realm of BDCs with stellar performance, CSWC is a top contender.

As an income investor with income as a primary goal - and portfolio value sustainability and growth as a secondary one - CSWC and its peers have provided strong and recurring income for a long time. I expect them to continue to do so.

This outpouring of income overwhelms my expenses and douses the flames of financial worry and anxiety. Retirement is a time to spend benefiting from all of the hard work that you have put in - that means exploring hobbies, traveling, or simply spending time with a loved one. If your portfolio is holding you back because of how you built it over the last decades, now is a perfect time to adjust that portfolio - so that it supports you in your retirement dreams instead of holding you back. If you're still working towards your retirement, make sure your portfolio is a tool that helps you leverage the best possible retirement ever, instead of simply having to be an albatross that you're feeding money into, hoping it gains value over time.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Very Bad News For Closed-End Funds
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

One of the main opportunities in closed-end funds comes from the discount/premium mechanisms that are naturally occurring in these investment structures. The share price can trade wildly different from the underlying net asset value per share. That can create some situations where there are some really good opportunities in these investment wrappers.

As a reminder, CEFs aren't an asset class or sector representation themselves. They are simply what their underlying portfolio can provide exposure to, which means they can provide some fairly diverse exposure. That includes equity funds, fixed-income exposure and even hybrid investments, which take a multi-asset approach between both equity and fixed-income. In addition to that, CEFs can take a specific sector exposure or a hybrid exposure to multiple sectors.

From there, it is important to note that not all premiums are necessarily a negative for a CEF, which is similar to the idea that not all discounts necessarily mean that a CEF is worth buying-even when they are trading at deep discounts of 40%+ on some occasions. That is, when one can invest in a fund at 60 cents on the dollar, it doesn't mean that it is a worthwhile investment.

Instead, we look for opportunities for CEFs that trade at wider-than-usual discounts or funds trading at relatively lower premiums compared to their history. That means that on some occasions, that can even include funds trading at a premium, which means the share price is above the NAV per share of the underlying portfolio.

As we enter 2025 and even cross through the first month (time moves fast when we are having fun!), it can be appropriate to look at overall valuations in the CEF space. In this case, we are unfortunately looking at discounts narrowing broadly across the space. Some funds are pushing into historically elevated discount/premium territory-and as we'll see below, the fixed-income space has really started to become quite elevated, historically speaking. This is a reversal from 2022 and 2023 when we were looking at historically wide discounts.

The market has delivered back-to-back solid gains into 2023 and 2024, which was after what was a fairly volatile 2022. This was particularly true for the mega-cap tech space and, even more specifically, the Magnificent 7 names.

With that, the broader market has simultaneously pushed CEFs to trade at historically narrow discounts now. This is a bit of a phenomenon where the broader overall market seems to influence CEFs. CEFs tend to be largely purchased by retail investors.

That doesn't mean that CEFs are devoid entirely of institutional investors, but some limitations can occur. The size of CEFs is relatively smaller, which has an influence on the daily trading volume. Being "closed" means that new shares aren't so easily issued as they can be with their exchange-traded fund counterparts-though there are ways to grow the overall outstanding shares through dividend reinvestment plans, at-the-market offerings, secondary offerings and rights offerings. These can both be accretive and/or dilutive, which depends on whether shares are issued at premiums or discounts, though that is a different subject for another time.

With that said, an institutional investor is often a larger entity managing billions in total capital, and for those reasons, it can limit participation from these larger investors when most CEFs are sub-$1 billion AUM. They could have a massive influence and easily become controlling shareholders as their stakes become majority holdings. That can also work for activist groups; as an example, Saba Capital Management can often garner significant influence for not all that much capital being deployed. However, trying to buy and sell such large blocks and be difficult due to liquidity constraints.

Therefore, these types of securities are mostly influenced by retail investors. Retail investors tend to get overly enthusiastic when times are good, but then turn to selling based on emotion when times are tough. Even while that should probably be the opposite of how investors should respond in the different scenarios, Warren Buffett's: "I will tell you how to become rich. Close the doors. Be fearful when others are greedy. Be greedy when others are fearful."

Simply put, it is much easier emotionally to invest in something when the price keeps rising (i.e., others are being greedy) compared to when investors are continuing to sell as a share price keeps declining (i.e., investors are being fearful.)

Given that, it comes to where we are in closed-end funds overall today, where the discounts have narrowed materially in the last year, and that has continued into 2025. Though we aren't that deep into 2025 yet, valuations for CEFs have remained on a narrowing trend more broadly, and that could mean tempering expectations going forward.

On average, all CEFs have seen discounts wider 46% of the time at the end of January 31, 2025, according to RiverNorth's data. That could otherwise actually suggest there could be plenty of areas of investing opportunity left.

However, discounts have narrowed even further since just the end of 2024 after we saw them declining through the year. As of February 10th, 2025, closing data, the average discount has come down to -2.44%.

This tightening of discounts was even more dramatic when we looked at the average discount at the end of 2023, which was -9.6%. That makes it all much more of a dramatic shift over the last year.

In looking at the average discount/premium level based on asset types of CEFs, equity is showing that, on average, there is a bit less value out there. In fact, as of February 10, 2025, they actually show an average discount -1.13%.

Worth noting is that one of the big outliers here is the Destiny Tech 100 (DXYZ), which can be really skewing the data with its nearly 900% premium listed on CEFConnect. Though worth noting is that they only provide a NAV quarterly.

Then where some opportunities might be in the multi-sector or hybrid allocation funds. On average, the discounts in this category comes in at -9.08%. That can include funds such as Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT), Nuveen Multi-Asset Income (NMAI) and Calamos Global Dynamic Income Fund (CHW), all trading at fairly attractive discount levels.

There is also Bexil Investment Trust (OTCPK:BXSY), they are listed as a hybrid but is almost entirely invested in equities. That fund trades at a deep discount, pushing out to nearly 38%, which can really skew the discount of the hybrid listed funds. BXSY is an interesting one, though some of the discounts could be considered deserved, as we covered previously. There is also the Highland Global Allocation Fund (HGLB) listed in the hybrid category and coming in at a discount that is similarly deep, pushing to a nearly 37% level.

With that said, in the taxable fixed-income space, we are seeing even more elevated valuations across the board as well. Going back to 1996, discounts have been wider 77% of the time. Making them historically quite richly valued. The average discount of the latest data on February 10, 2025, comes in at a similarly shallow -0.42%.

Of course, in the fixed-income space, we have the PIMCO funds, which notoriously skew the data due to regularly trading at premiums. This isn't all that unusual, so it isn't necessarily a negative-based on the 1-year z-score, some of these are even looking relatively attractive.

Name Ticker Premium/Discount 1-Year Z-score
PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (PFN) 5.26% 1.1
PIMCO Income Strategy (PFL) 4.56% 0.32
PIMCO High Income (PHK) 5.30% -0.03
PIMCO Global StocksPLUS & Income (PGP) 0.50% -0.51
PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX) -11.60% -0.66
PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) 6.23% 1.59
PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) 14.05% 0.87
PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy (PCN) 15.34% -0.18
PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) 25.89% 0.03
PIMCO Access Income (PAXS) 6.09% 1

That said, we do have the more hybrid allocation fund PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX), which does offer a hefty discount. This fund is still more of a hybrid fund due to its heavy focus on energy investments, primarily Venture Global (VG) being a huge position.

While equities and taxable fixed-income are looking relatively more expensive, muni funds are looking like the best value when we look at the long-term history. However, in looking at the current average discount, it has narrowed over the last month when compared to the 2024 end. More recently, the average discount has moved to -6.95%-still a relative bargain.

That doesn't mean that there aren't still some pockets of opportunity or that CEFs will more broadly perform poorly in terms of total returns. As we noted, it isn't appropriate to put CEFs in a small box; as a single type of asset or sector, they can be invested in various areas of the market, and the underlying portfolio is important to be aware of.

That said, on an average basis, it could become more difficult going forward for CEF investors because of the historical narrowing of CEFs across the board. With that, the main takeaway is trying to be more selective when investing in CEFs overall.

Conclusion

CEFs often deliver relatively higher distribution rates to investors who choose to invest in these investment structures. CEFs can provide significant opportunities, but there are some meaningful differences that investors should familiarize themselves with that aren't always represented in other investment structures. While CEFs aren't representative of asset classes or sectors themselves specifically, each fund can be individually focused on different areas, meaning each CEF investment can perform quite differently.

In general, today, we looked to highlight why investors should be more picky about where they are choosing to put capital. We previously highlighted what risks there are in CEFs more broadly before investing in this space. We've seen discounts narrow across the board, which means that investors can't just blindly throw darts at a board. Being more patient or more selective in where an investor chooses to put capital to work in CEFs has a better chance of performing better going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Bulls Underestimate Headwinds Ahead
data.description:
JPM stock: previous thesis and Q4 recap

My last work on JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM) was published in November 2024, as you can see from the following screenshot. That article was titled "Bank of America And JPMorgan Chase: Deregulation Tailwinds Ahead". As the title already suggested, the article argued for a buy rating on JPM due to my anticipation of policy tailwinds with the outcome of the presidential election (which was announced in Nov 2024). Quote:

JPM and BAC have benefited from a sharp rally post-election, both outperforming the average financial sector. I will argue JPM and BAC rallies are far from over given the potential deregulation expected from the upcoming Trump administration. Leading banks like BAC and JPM are especially well-positioned to benefit due to their scale, profitability, and financial strength. Their current P/E ratios admittedly appear elevated, but investors should also examine other valuation metrics (such as dividend yields). All told, some valuation premium is well justified given the potential for sustained growth ahead.

Since then, there have been a few important catalysts evolving around JPM. In the remainder of this article, I will elaborate on the top 2 on my list: the policy tailwinds and also JPM's Q4 earnings report (ER). My assessment is that the policy tailwinds I saw at the time of my last writing have already been priced in by this time, thus obsoleting my previous thesis. Furthermore, I also think its Q4 ER results are unusually good and helped to make JPM bulls underestimate the uncertainties ahead. For these reasons, this article downgrades my rating on the stock to HOLD.

For readers unfamiliar with the Q4 ER, the chart below shows a summary of the key figures. Its Q4 earnings were announced on January 15, 2025, and showed a super quarter as seen. Both Normalized EPS and GAAP EPS surpassed expectations by a wide margin (of around 16%). Revenue for the quarter reached $42.77B, also exceeding estimates by $1.20B. Digging into the financials a bit more, a large contributing factor was a 33.7% jump in non-interest revenue, reflecting increases in such categories as investment banking fees, principal transactions, asset management fees, and card income. What's more, the total provision for credit losses dropped 4.7%. Finally, the share number diminished thanks to share buybacks and also helped boost the EPS somewhat.

Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter (scheduled for April 11, 2025, pre-market), the market expects a good show also, as reflected by the domination of upward EPS revisions in the last 90 days (12 upward revisions vs. 0 downward revisions).

However, I think these expectations underestimated the uncertainties ahead, as to be argued next.

JPM stock: earnings outlook and uncertainties ahead

First, judging by the latest inflation and job data, I expect inflationary headwinds to persist or even worsen in the next future quarters. This could lead to an increase in its total provision for credit losses and offset the decrease reported in Q4. As a byproduct of inflation, the interest rate outlook has also become cloudier than at the time of my last writing. As an example, the following CME Group FedWatch Tool compares the current probability of rate cuts between now and Jan 2026 (i.e., in about 1 year). As seen, currently, the market's expectations are pretty spread out. The most expected scenario is for the Fed rates to be further lowered to the 3.75% to 4% range. But the odds are only 32.7%. There are few other scenarios with more or less aggressive cuts with almost equal odds. Such a large uncertainty makes it difficult to predict (or for JPM to plan) the earnings from its various segments.

Another risk that has been developing since my last writing is geopolitical developments, notably the ongoing conflicts in Ukraine, the Middle East, and also the potential of an escalation of tariff disputes. All these developments could threaten to inflict great damage on the world economy.

Other risks and final thoughts

As an additional risk, the stock is now valued at a premium by my assessment, and thus both heightening the valuation risk and also reducing the potency of future share buybacks (if any). To wit, the next chart shows some of its specific valuation metrics. As seen, JPM receives a D- for its overall valuation grade. There are indeed good reasons behind such an off-putting rating when I looked more closely at the underlying metrics. JPM's current P/E ratios are significantly higher compared to both the sector median and also its own 5-year average. For instance, the TTM Non-GAAP P/E is 13.39, which is 11.18% higher than the sector median of 12.04 and 21.50% higher than its 5-year average of 11.02. Similarly, the FWD Non-GAAP P/E is 14.44, representing a 28.81% premium over the sector median and an 18.28% premium over its 5-year average.

All told, I don't see anything wrong with holding a money-center bank, even at a premium valuation. I still expect the current Trump administration to maintain a business-friendly environment, and JPMorgan is well positioned to benefit. Finally, its financial strength is in excellent shape. As of the last quarter, JPM's equity tier 1 capital ratio sits at 15.7%, relative to the 2023 figure of 15.0%. Leverage and liquidity coverage ratios were extremely healthy in my view. However, I just do not see the same degree of skewness in its return/risk curve anymore due to the uncertainties that have been developing since my last writing. As such, this article downgrades my rating to HOLD.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report February 22, 2025
data.description:
In this brief market report, we look at the various asset classes, sectors, equity categories, ETFs, and stocks that moved the market higher and the market segments that defied the trend by moving lower.

Identifying the pockets of strength and weakness allows us to see the direction of significant money flows and their origin.

After making a new high Wednesday, the market stumbles.

How bad was Friday's drubbing? In the context of historical daily returns it wasn't that bad. But it certainly gets your attention.

A look at monthly returns.

This chart shows the monthly returns for the past year. Friday's decline knocked us into the red for February. Since Trump was elected, the market is up by 1.4%.

A look at drawdowns this year.

Here is a closer look at the pullbacks we've had over the last 12 months, using a drawdown chart. The current drawdown is 2.1% after making a new high on Wednesday.

A look at the bull run since it began last October.

This chart highlights the 68.1% gain in the S&P 500 from the October 2022 low through Friday's close. We came to rest below the trendline and it looks like we may have further to go before this pullback is over.

Major asset class performance.

Here is a look at the performance of the major asset classes, sorted by last week's returns. I also included the returns since the October 12, 2022 low for additional context.

The best performer last week was Asia, led by China. The worst performer was Blockchain, as Bitcoin ended the week at $95,600. That's down from the recent high water mark of $108,100.

Equity sector performance

For this report I use the expanded sectors as published by Zacks. They use 16 sectors rather than the standard 11. This gives us added granularity as we survey the winners and losers.

Investors were buying defensive sectors like Utilities and Consumer Staples while selling Retail and Transportation stocks.

Equity group performance

For the groups, I separate the stocks in the S&P 1500 Composite Index by shared characteristics like growth, value, size, cyclical, defensive, and domestic vs. foreign.

The best-performing groups last week were Emerging Market stocks and US Defensive stocks. Investors raised cash by selling Small and Midcap Growth and the Mag 7.

The S&P Mag 7

Here is a look at the seven mega-cap stocks that have been leading the market over the past two years. These seven stocks are off to a weak start YTD. Faith in the AI trade is being tested. Participation in the bull market has broadened on a year to date basis. META was hardest hit, while AAPL managed to eke out a small gain for the week.

The S&P Top 7 dominance is reversing itself.

After leading the market higher for the last two years, the Mag 7 are now a drag on the S&P 500 index on a year-to-date basis. 66% of all stocks in the S&P 1500 were down last week, while 30% were up and 4% were unchanged.

The 10 best-performing ETFs from last week

The two biggest winners this week - Emerging Market internet stocks and Gold - demonstrate how market leadership is changing in 2025.

The 10 worst-performing ETFs from last week

It's no surprise to see the ARK ETFs taking the #1 and #2 slots on the worst performing ETF list, as these funds tend to bounce from big losses one week to big gains the next. Investing in ARK funds is not for the faint of heart.

Best-Performing Stocks

Celsius (CELH) spiked on better than expected earnings, and a $1.8 Billion acquisition.

Worst-Performing Stocks

Trupanion Inc. (TRUP) lost 1/3 of its value after an earnings miss.

Final thoughts

To recap, in the week just past, investors were:

  • Selling small cap growth and buying large cap value
  • Selling Blockchain and buying China stocks
  • Selling Fintech and buying Gold
  • Selling Retail and buying Utilities
  • Selling US stocks and buying Foreign stocks

My view is that long term momentum is still healthy but there will be pullbacks like this along the way - maybe even a 10% correction - until we get more clarity about tariffs and inflation. Therefore I'm advising clients to wait for deeper pullbacks to put fresh cash to work.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AIEQ: An Example Of AI Failure
data.description:
This article updates my review of AIEQ published in July 2024, in light of current holdings and recent performance.

AIEQ strategy

Amplify AI Powered Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:AIEQ) was launched on 10/17/2017 and tracks the AI Powered Equity Index. AIEQ has a portfolio of 157 stocks, an expense ratio of 0.75% and an extremely high portfolio turnover rate (1159% in the most recent fiscal year!). The fund has been reorganized in 2023, keeping the same ticker and objective.

As described in the prospectus by Amplify ETFs, the eligible stock universe is composed of the holdings of iShares Core S&P Total U.S. Stock Market ETF (ITOT). The selection and weighing process is powered by a deep learning model developed by EquBot Inc running on IBM's Watson platform. It takes into account the different aspects of the market: fundamental, technical, sentiment and economy to predict the expected price return of a stock over a one-month horizon. Then, it selects and assign weights to a subset of stocks within the index universe to build the portfolio with the highest one-month forecasted return under some allocation constraints. In particular, weights are capped at 10% for constituents, and 25% for industries (including SPACs and REITs). The index is reconstituted monthly. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 index, represented by SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Deep learning in simple words

Deep learning is a branch of artificial intelligence using software components inspired from the human brain: neural networks. They are used to identify, extract and combine features from historical data to make predictions with new data. The learning process involves multiple iterations of training and testing, which result in an operational predictive model. A model is typically defined by a graph of nodes and connections associated with parameters and organized in multiple layers. They can be metaphorically considered as neurons and synapses of an artificial brain.

The number of layers and nodes in each layer may vary. The prediction process consists in feeding a trained model with new input data to classify elements in categories with associated probabilities, or calculate expected values.

Portfolio

The sector breakdown may vary from one month to the next and is not typical of the strategy. In my latest review of AIEQ in July, I reported that consumer discretionary was the heaviest sector, with 24% of asset value. Now, the portfolio is overweight in technology (35.4%) and has significant exposure in healthcare (17.7%). Other sectors are below 12%.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are reasonable. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 45.5% of asset value and the nine largest positions weigh between 4% and 6%. However, this table changes every month and weights may be higher: Tesla was over 9% in July.

Performance

Since 10/24/2017, AIEQ has underperformed SPY by 6.5% in annualized return. Additionally, the maximum drawdown and standard deviation of monthly return point to a higher risk. The risk-adjusted performance of AIEQ (measured by Sharpe ratio in the table below) is less than half of SPY's.

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of AIEQ and five other AI-powered ETFs:

AIEQ is in second position behind AIVL for assets under management and liquidity in dollar volume. The three other funds are very small and thinly traded. The next chart compares total returns, starting on 5/21/2019 and excluding NEWZ and LQAI, which have less than two years of track record. AIEQ beats AIVL, but is far behind QRFT and AMOM.

Takeaway

Amplify AI Powered Equity ETF holds 157 stocks selected and weighted every month with an AI model. The sector breakdown, top holdings and aggregate fundamental ratios may change from one month to the next, making any attempt of fundamental analysis questionable (if not useless). AIEQ has steadily and significantly underperformed the S&P 500 since its inception, and shows higher risk metrics. Moreover, it has lagged two other AI-powered funds since 2019. I am not an AI-basher. In fact, I hold a PhD in computer science (1994) and passed a Machine Learning Engineer certification in 2022 to keep pace with the technology. Nonetheless, I think AIEQ is a waste of AI resources and I renew my Sell rating due to lackluster return, high volatility, high fees and excessive turnover. A note of caution, though: this ETF doesn't look good enough to hold it, but it is unpredictable enough to refrain from shorting it.

data.title: Nexa Resources S.A. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Nexa Resources S.A. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: The Reasons Digital Realty Is Not Worth The Current Price
data.description:
Digital Realty (NYSE:DLR) is a real estate investment trust that specializes in data centers. We have started coverage of the company back in August 2023, with an initial hold rating, emphasizing the firm's strong track of dividend payment, as well as its high levels of debt and high valuation. Later, in May 2024, we have downgraded our rating to sell, again highlighting the overvaluation based on a dividend discount model, and a set of traditional price multiples.

Since our last article, DLR has actually returned over 19%, outperforming the broader market. In our article today, we are going to discuss, whether this outperformance is likely to last, and whether our previous analysis of the stock's fair value was overly pessimistic or not. We are once again going to use a dividend discount model, but we will update the main input parameters to reflect the most up-to-date data. We are also going to highlight the key messages from the latest quarterly earnings report.

Valuation update

In our previous writing, we have estimated DLR's fair value to be between $90 to $116 per share, using a 9% required rate of return and a dividend growth rate of 2.5% and 4.4%, respectively. Back then, the share price was $142, so our calculations suggested a significant downside potential.

Today, we are going to use again a dividend discount model, but with updated input parameters. Today, our required rate of return is going to be 6.9%, based on the latest estimate of the firm's weighted average cost of capital (WACC). This value is significantly lower, than the previously used 9%, which is going to positively influence our estimated fair value.

The previously assumed dividend growth rates, on the other hand, now appear to be somewhat optimistic and will be adjusted downwards to 1.8% for a low case, and 3.9% for a high case, in perpetuity.

Also, important to mention that currently DLR's annual dividend is $4.88, corresponding to a yield of 2.8%.

Based on these updated input parameters, our estimated fair values for a high- and low case are $174 and $102 per share.

Compared to the current share price, the high case represents an upside potential of roughly 5%, while the low case represents a downside potential of approximately 40%.

So what do these values mean? In order to make them meaningful, and decide whether the low- or high-case is more likely, we have to give them some context.

Interpretation of results

1. The need for data centers in today's world is inevitable. As more and more people and companies are using AI, the need for computational power is bigger than ever. DLR is well-positioned to house these data centers. As demand is growing strongly, DLR is also in a good position to hike rental rates, which is highlighted in their latest earnings presentation as well.

The firm's robust backlog further implies growth ahead, and a potential argument for a higher valuation.

2. The firm has also managed to significantly reduce its leverage. This is also an important - and good - development, as we have earlier indicated that the high leverage, coupled with the high interest rate environment, might hurt DLR's financial results.

3. On the other hand, not everything perfect with DLR. While revenue increased by 5.1% YoY to $1.44B, it missed analyst estimates by $20 million. FFO, however, came in at $1.73 per share, or $0.02 above analyst expectations. The issued guidance for revenue also appears to have disappointed analysts. DLR expects revenue for 2025 to be in the range of $5.800B-$5.900B, while analysts were expecting $6.13B.

4. While DLR's FFO has been growing over the past years, they have not increased their dividends significantly in the last three years, which may not be attractive for dividend- and dividend growth investors.

We also have to emphasize that during the same period, the company has also significantly diluted its shareholders by issuing new shares. We believe this also makes DLR unattractive.

5. Last, but not least, DLR is still selling at a significant premium compared to the broader REIT sector, as well as to its own historical averages. The following table contains a set of traditional price multiples to illustrate this overvaluation.

One might argue that DLR's growth justifies the valuation, but when we compare the growth metrics with those of the sector median, we do not see that DLR's figures are overwhelmingly better.

All in all, we believe that starting a position at the current price is too risky. We would like to see the share price pull back so that there is enough of margin of safety, which could justify an investment for a value- or dividend investor.

Key takeaways

Our updated estimated fair value range, based on a single stage dividend discount model, is $102 to $174, assuming a required rate of return of 6.9% and dividend growth rates of 1.8% and 3.9% respectively.

While the demand for data centers is increasing - as indicated by the firm's strong backlog, as well as by the strong pricing environment - the issued revenue guidance was softer than expected by analysts.

The firm has managed to reduce its leverage over the past two years, which is a significant piece of good news in the current interest rate environment. At the same time, however, DLR has also diluted its shareholders by issuing new shares.

The firm's valuation based on a set of traditional price multiples also appears unattractive.

While we do not believe that buying at the current price levels is attractive, our previous sell rating might have been too pessimistic.

We currently rate DLR's stock as hold.

data.title: The Ultimate Trade War Hedge: Why I Trust CTA With 56% Of My Portfolio
data.description:
The trade war has been ongoing since Jan. 30 when news broke that tariffs were imminent.

The stock market is actually up 0.6% since the trade war began, with big tech up over 2%.

Except for KMLM, most hedging assets are up this year.

In fact, despite the trade war, which seems to be worsening by the week, every asset class is up this year.

  • Making KMLM's negative return (after negative returns in 2023 and 2024) even more impressive (in a bad way).

Let's examine the latest news about the worsening trade war. Then, I'll explain the three reasons why the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:CTA) is my No. 1 recommendation for hedging.

I've been buying more CTA every week since the trade war began.

I Just Bought 2,000 More CTA.

Trade War Update: Things Are Still Getting Worse

So... 10% extra tariffs on Chinese imports are now in effect, but no other tariffs have gone up... yet.

This schedule doesn't even include the reciprocal tariffs that I covered in my last economic report in detail.

  • 3 Shocking Economic Facts That Could Make You A Lot Of Money

The latest tariffs will take effect on April 2. They will start at 25% and rise "very substantially higher" over one year.

The pharma and chip tariffs are expected to be phased in over the rest of the year.

The hope of a Canada/Mexico-style postponement of tariffs from some concessions is likely why the stock market increased during this trade war.

However, the EU is talking "firm countermeasures," implying they might go tit-for-tat (25% tariffs matching our 25% tariffs).

Metric Impact
U.S. GDP (2025) -0.4% (Goldman Sachs estimate if tariffs persist)
S&P 500 Earnings -2–3% (Goldman Sachs forecast)
Core Inflation +0.7 ppt (Goldman Sachs estimate)
Auto Industry Profits Toyota/Honda: -33% to -50% (Bloomberg Intelligence)
Nvidia Implied Move ±7–8% (Feb 27 options pricing)

(Sources: WSJ, Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg)

NVIDIA (NVDA) would potentially be significantly impacted by 25% to 100% tariffs (as Trump has previously mentioned) on chips, impacting its margins.

Currently, the options around earnings are pricing in a 7.5% move in either direction.

  • NVDA is capable of 20% daily gains or drops as we've seen in the last year.
Sector Key Vulnerabilities Exposed Companies
Autos Import-dependent supply chains; EU/Japan retaliation risks Toyota, Honda, BMW, Ford, GM
Pharmaceuticals Higher drug costs; potential shortages in generics/APIs Pfizer, Merck, J&J
Semiconductors Disrupted Asian manufacturing (TSMC); margin compression for U.S. designers Nvidia, AMD, Apple, Qualcomm

(Sources: WSJ, Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg)

Other tech companies that could be affected by the chip tariff include AMD (AMD), Apple (AAPL), and QUALCOMM (QCOM).

The pharma tariffs potentially could impact normally defensive blue chips such as Merck (MRK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).

Given that I am almost 13.5% Nvidia you might think I'm losing sleep over the potential for these chip tariffs.

So far, there's no evidence that President Trump will postpone chip tariffs beyond April 2, even for companies like TSMC, which is spending $65 billion to build chip plants in the US (Arizona).

These plants will be completed in 2027 (at the earliest) because it takes three to four years (no matter how much money is spent) to build some of the most expensive and complex buildings humans have ever designed.

No tariffs can speed up the process of completing US chip factories.

I fully expect TSMC will get an exception from tariffs (the No. 1 supplier for NVDA, QCOM, and AMD).

  • TSMC builds 90% of AI chips in Taiwan.
  • US plants can't replace these chips for several years.

After all, they're already investing $65 billion into the US and Jensen Huang recently visited the White House.

  • Mexico's President and Canada's Prime Minister merely had to call the President on the phone to get deals done.

President Trump said that DeepSeek showed that the US must step up its game in the AI race with China. The $500 billion Project Stargate shows that Trump is a big proponent of US AI exceptionalism.

These tariffs are entirely counterproductive for US AGI development and thus won't happen.

Tim Culpan, a Taipei-based former Bloomberg Opinion columnist covering technology in Asia, said in an online opinion piece on Friday that if Trump goes further than standalone chips and adds import duties to a broader category of semiconductor products including GPUs, modules and subsystems, then the results could be "disastrous."

"The U.S. doesn't have the capacity to manufacture anywhere near a similar volume of equivalent devices. It would take months, if not years, to build the factories, spin up the tools, and then go through the qualification process," he wrote in the article titled "How a TSMC Tax Would be a FAFO Move for the US."

In the opinion piece, Culpan argues that the U.S. could impose a tax on Taiwanese chips, but it would hurt the very companies Trump used to bolster his tech credentials." - Focus Taiwan

Sector Rate Effective Date Key Conditions
Autos 25% April 2, 2025 EU negotiations; domestic auto-lobbying
Pharmaceuticals 25%+ TBD (2025–2026) Possible exemptions for critical medicines
Semiconductors 25%+ TBD (2025–2026) TSMC Arizona plant delays (2027 completion)

(Sources: WSJ, Goldman Sachs, Bloomberg)

Still, what happens if these tariffs do go into effect?

  • Trade War Tactics: How I'm Protecting My Life Savings From The Worst-Case Scenario

I already covered the worst-case scenarios based on the best available data and economic models in this special report.

A whole year of trade war would likely result in a 28% decline in US stocks, with market declines ranging from 15% to 44%.

Why am I not overly concerned about a year-long trade war?

Simple, follow the money.

S&P Decline $ Market Cap Loss Wealth Decline for Top 10% (93%) Wealth Decline for Top 1% (54%)
15% $9.23 trillion $8.58 trillion $4.98 trillion
24% $14.77 trillion $13.74 trillion $7.98 trillion
28% $17.23 trillion $16.02 trillion $9.31 trillion
37% $22.77 trillion $21.18 trillion $12.30 trillion
44% $27.08 trillion $25.19 trillion $14.63 trillion

(Sources: Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley)

The US stock market is worth $61 trillion; the richest 10% owns 93% of stocks, and the top 1% owns 54% of US stocks.

Here's the risk analysis table I showed before, but converted to the wealth destruction for the top 10% and 1%.

A one-year trade war will likely cost the top 1% (who hold the political power) close to $10 trillion.

Whether or not you believe the concentration of political power in the hands of the rich is good, it exists, and I'm confident that the wealthiest 10% won't allow their net worth to decline by $16 trillion over the next year.

OK, but what if I'm wrong? What if President Trump doesn't care about what the richest 1% or 10% want and believes that a one or even two-year global trade war is necessary?

Well, that's why I'm buying CTA every week in this trade war, and it already represents 56% of my portfolio.

  • Approximately 51% of my net worth.

Reason One: Simplify Managed Futures Is The King Of Hedging Assets

There are many ways to hedge a portfolio, but historically (in the last six bear markets), managed futures were the No. 1 strategy. During bear markets, managed futures had an average gain of 32%, mirroring the S&P's 32% decline.

In other words, since 1990, if you had used managed futures as your hedging asset, you could have achieved remarkably low volatility.

Any 50% or more allocation of managed futures would have resulted in a portfolio that created portfolios that went up in the average bear market of the last 35 years.

But there's something even more important than how much a hedging asset goes up in a bear market.

Remember that corrections are much more common than bear markets, which average one per six years. And 5% to 9.9% pullbacks are even more common.

There have been 23 pullbacks that the market has suffered since the GFC bottom.

Most hedging assets are not actually good hedges, as you can see here.

If something falls less than the market in a correction, that's OK, but for a hedge, I want something that goes up in a filing market.

The Downside Capture Ratio: The Most Important Hedging Metric You've Never Heard Of

The upside capture ratio is the percent of market gains in a rising market that your portfolio (or some asset) captures.

The downside capture ratio is the percent of market declines in a filing market that your portfolio (or some asset) captures.

The ideal theoretical hedging asset would have close to (or higher) -100% downside capture.

  • Goes up the mirror image (or more) of the falling market.

Since 1990, during the last six bear markets, managed futures (Mount Lucas index specifically) have had such a downside capture ratio.

But what about the last few years? What if the hedges that work overtime change over time? What if things like bonds that worked well for 40 years no longer work well?

Hedging Options Ranked By Downside Capture Ratio

Hedging Asset Asset Class Ticker Downside Capture Ratio Since March 8th, 2022
Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF Managed Futures CTA -88.12
AQR Managed Futures Strategy Fund Managed Futures AQMIX -79.66

KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy

 Managed Futures KMLM -66.13

AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy

 Managed Futures AMFAX -61.39
iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy Managed Futures DBMF -54.24
Emerging Markets Bond Fund - Institutional Class) Bonds EBSIX -53.82

PIMCO TRENDS Managed Futures Strategy

 Bonds PQTAX -37.38
Loomis Sayles Strategic Income Fund Bonds LOTIX -32.12
First Trust Managed Futures Strategy Fund ETF Managed Futures FMF -9.94
SPDR® Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill Bonds BIL -7.33
Direxion Auspice Broad Commodity Strategy ETF Commodities COM 9.05
SPDR® Gold Shares ETF Commodities GLD 10.72
WisdomTree Managed Futures Strategy Fund ETF Managed Futures WTMF 19.44
iShares® Commodity Curve Carry Strategy ETF Commodities CCRV 21.52
Abbey Capital Multi-Asset Fund Return Stacked/Managed Futures MAFIX 22.45
Invesco DB Commodity Index Tracking Fund ETF Commodities DBC 27.46
Standpoint Multi-Asset Fund Return Stacked/Managed Futures BLNDX 28.49
Convergence Long/Short Equity ETF Equity Hedge Fund Strategy CLSE 42.72
First Trust Long/Short Equity ETF Equity Hedge Fund Strategy FTLS 45.23
iShares Core U.S. Aggregate Bond Bonds AGG 52.12
RW Strategic Income Fund Bonds RWSIX 62.79
PIMCO 25+ Year Zero Coupon U.S. Treasury Index Bonds ZROZ 165.35
Average 0.78
Median 9.89
Min (Best) CTA -88.12
Max (Worst) ZROZ 165.35

(Source: Portfolio Visualizer)

In terms of what had the best hedging power, CTA is the clear winner, with managed futures taking the top five spots.

  • Average downside capture ratio for managed futures: -43%
  • Median downside capture ratio for managed futures: -52%

Reason Two: Simplify Is Best Performing Managed Futures Fund Since Inception

CTA is not only the best-performing managed futures fund of the last three years but also the No. 1 hedging asset in terms of positive total returns.

Gold came close, but CTA has delivered 42% total returns since inception, not much lower than the S&P or Nasdaq in a red-hot bull market.

OK, that's pretty cool, but why am I confident that trend-following managed futures will likely do well over time? Three years is not a significant period of time, and other than CTA, most other managed futures delivered pretty mediocre returns.

For example, Alpha Simplex, one of the largest and most respected managed futures managers is up 1% in the last three years, including distributions, so losing to inflation.

Actually, trend following has been found to be a highly reliable strategy for centuries.

The historical returns for the trend following is roughly that of the stock market itself, just with no correlation to the market (and the best hedging upside returns in bear markets).

OK, but isn't there a lot of risk being 100% in one ETF for one's hedging bucket? Isn't it true that managed futures managers have very little (or no) long-term alpha?

That's why DBMF is currently the second-largest managed futures ETF, with nearly $1.5 billion in assets.

  • DBMF replicates the top 20 managed futures funds.
  • The "Vanguard of managed futures."

Reason Three: Simplify's Rules-Based System Is Likely Better Than Its Peers

I spent several hours researching the specific mechanics of CTA before I bought it and reviewed these before writing this report.

Let me explain why I believe that CTA's superior historical returns (and hedging power) are likely to persist.

Managed futures are famous for their zero correlation to stocks and bonds over time.

CTA's correlation? -0.34.

Why? It's all in how CTA is built and managed.

CTA is designed to maximize long-term volatility-adjusted returns... and generate the strongest negative downside capture ratio (hedging power).

Management (Altis Partners runs the actual algos) started out in 2000 as a specialist in trend following and then added carry and relative value (income and value) to their algorithm over time.

According to management, the best historical returns (and hedging power) come from using pure commodities and bonds, and ignoring stocks and currencies.

That's why CTA only targets 32 highly liquid US and Canadian futures contracts in bonds and commodities.

Why would these asset classes make for better returns and superior hedging power over time?

Because commodities tend to whipsaw less, supply vs. demand drives commodity prices.

If there's a drought in Africa (the No. 1 grower of coffee), then coffee prices will soar.

No amount of political pressure can change this. There's no OPEC for coffee and no one that any politician can pressure to change coffee prices.

Coffee prices have more than doubled in the past year, with the most substantial run in recent weeks.

Natural gas has increased 164% in the last year and was on a seven-day winning streak, including a 20% rally in just five days.

Gold prices are at record highs, with a 46% gain in the last year.

Corn has been up 22% in the last year, but 37% in the last six months, and it has been up 5% since the trade war began.

  • Corn is a trade war-sensitive crop (tariffs will cause higher prices).

Wheat (another trade war-sensitive crop) is up over 11% since the trade war began.

In 2025, copper prices have been up almost 15%, including a 6% gain since the trade war began.

Why am I bringing up these commodities?

Because those are the largest holdings for CTA right now.

  • CTA updates its holdings daily and provides this table to the public each day.

The percentage breakdown of the CTA holdings is:

  • Commodity Futures: 93.09%

  • Bond Futures: 6.91%

Commodities are trending cleanly, with prices generally at two-year highs as markets prepare for a trade war.

Interest rates, though, usually trend cleanly, too. This is because central banks drive short-term rates and influence long-term rates, primarily driven by inflation and growth expectations.

  • Central banks (Fed and Bank of Canada) will not usually change policies quickly.
  • Growth and inflation expectations don't change overnight.

In other words, while the stock market and currency markets can change overnight (the Peso crashing after Trump won in November, for example), commodities and interest rates are historically the best approach to trend following.

In other words, managed futures that use stocks, currencies, commodities, and interest rates (bonds), most of which are "de-worsifying" as Buffett would say.

PIMCO's managed futures fund has more than 1,000 futures contracts, the king of "diversification."

And it's down 10% in the last three years, while CTA is the No. 1 performer because it's concentrated on the two top asset classes for trend following.

More Than Just Trend Following and Concentration

Wait a second. CTA's red-hot returns appear to be due to "gunslinger"-like concentration (up to 25% of the portfolio in a single futures contract).

Isn't that dangerous? It would be, except that CTA isn't just trend following.

Commodities are trend followed using multiple time horizons and weighted to optimize negative correlation with the stock market.

CTA uses a relative value and carry strategy (value and futures roll yield) to protect against extremes.

  • AlphaSimplex can occasionally go 800% weighting if the trend is strong enough.
  • CTA is capped at 25%, no matter how strong the trend is.

Here's an example provided by the CIO of CTA.

In example one, a commodity is trending strongly (soaring) and is overvalued.

So, CTA would go long, but not as long as other funds that use pure trend following (like KMLM).

In example two, the trend is strongly up, and the commodity is historically undervalued.

  • Roll yield is higher than the historical average.

So CTA would go extra-long for this commodity, more so than its peers would.

In example three, the trend is highly negative (falling price), but the roll yield shows that the valuation is so good that CTA would effectively go neutral and not take a significant position either way.

How This Plays Out In Real Time

Back in December, this is what CTA's portfolio looked like.

The top holding was cocoa, thanks to prices that had soared almost 200% in a year due to severe drought in Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana (the two largest producers).

Cocoa prices were up 200% at their Dec. 18 peak (over 12 months).

On Dec. 12, CTA was 17% Cocoa. It peaked on Dec. 18 at 25% cocoa (the risk cap).

From Oct. 24 to Dec. 18, cocoa doubled in price.

CTA caught that trend and rode to the maximum overweight cocoa.

But then what happened?

Cocoa fell 15% by Dec. 27. In just nine days, CTA's largest position (a maximum 25% risk cap) crashed.

A 15% decrease in 25% of the portfolio should have dropped CTA by 3.75%.

It didn't because CTA was 75% other futures, and as cocoa was peaking, CTA began quickly reducing its position size.

Focus on nat gas and the 16% volatility (position sizing).

On Feb. 18, nat gas jumped 8%.

On Feb. 17, nat gas peaked at 23% of CTA.

After an 8% gain (when most peers were doubling down), CTA actually pulled back to 18%.

And on Feb. 19, nat gas soared 6% more.

Its peers were piling into natural gas, and the trend said, "Longer and longer, ride the wave!"

And CTA? It pulled back to 16% nat gas, which fell to the second-biggest position.

CTA didn't market time the reversal in nat gas; they simply followed their algo, which said that on a carry and relative value basis, natural gas was overvalued, and the trend might reverse soon.

What about cocoa? Remember how that was 25% of the portfolio on Dec. 18?

What is cocoa now? 1.5%, and instead of being 30% long, is cocoa (Dec. 18 peak) CTA? -0.9% long (neutral).

In other words, CTA's algorithm is designed to capture quicker trends and lock in profits rather than ride a trend and blindly go longer and longer into a bubble or shorter and shorter into a historical crash.

The 3 Reasons CTA Is Likely To Keep Outperforming Its Peers (Summary)

  1. The two cleanest trending asset classes (commodities and bonds) - No "de-worsification."
  2. Trend, Carry, and Relative Value (momentum, income, and value).
  3. A fast-changing dynamic algo that rides strong trends and locks in profits (without forcing investors to time, CTA does it for you).

I'm not a market timer, so I cannot tell you whether gold, natural gas, or coffee are good investments right now.

  • Top 3 Positions (Coffee, Natural Gas, Gold):

    • 46.06% of CTA.

    • My portfolio is 25% long coffee, natural gas, and gold.

  • Top 6 Positions (adding Soybean Meal, Corn, and Wheat):

    • 65.60% of CTA.

    • My portfolio is 37% long coffee, natural gas, gold, soybean meal, corn, and wheat).

  • First Top Positions (adding Copper, WTI Crude, HRW Wheat, and Cotton):

    • 80.91% of CTA.
    • My portfolio is 45% long coffee, natural gas, gold, soybean meal, corn, wheat, copper, and short oil and cotton).

My portfolio is 44% stocks and 45% long or short commodities.

Commodities tend to increase in trade wars because many are imported.

Risk Profile: Why CTA Isn't Right For Everyone

OK, so CTA is the No. 1 hedging asset over its first three years, with the highest negative downside capture ratio.

And it's the best-performing hedging asset, too.

That's potentially by design since management directly designed it to use the two most effective hedging asset classes (commodities and bonds). It also used a concentrated approach to risk management (25% risk cap) and combined value and income (carry and relative roll yield) to minimize the risk of giving up profits from mega commodity rallies.

Historical Returns Since April 2022

The best returns, the best hedging power! And not due to luck but because CTA was explicitly designed to deliver... the best returns... and the best hedging power.

Management has delivered exactly what was promised.

But that doesn't mean CTA makes you feel like a genius all the time.

On any given day, a concentrated commodity position can result in a sharp down day (natural gas was down 5% at one point on Feb. 20, and CTA was down as much as 1.7% that day.

OK, that's just one day, though.

CTA does suffer if there's no strong trending pattern from commodities (2023) or if there's a sudden "black swan event."

2022 was the worst bond bear market in history. So, what did trend followers do? Short bonds! To the max!

CTA has risk caps and relative value and carry yield breaks, but when did the bond yield go from 0.5% to 4%? That was merely a return to historically normal bond yields.

So the "risk break" didn't work, and CTA was max short bonds.

And then the Silicon Valley bank imploded overnight, and suddenly bonds had their strongest rally since 1982.

Managed futures were the most short bonds in their history... at the exact wrong time.

It was the worst weekly decline in industry history, with some managed futures funds falling as much as 25% (and closing after decades of solid returns).

CTA was the No. 1 performing fund of 2023 going into the crisis, up almost 20%.

And it then fell 17% in a week.

CTA went from "I feel like a genius for owning God's hedging asset!" to "OMG, this is broken!" By the end of the year, it had mostly recovered.

But for 2023? Investors lost 2%, in a year when stocks soared 26%.

As you can see, in 2024 and 2022 CTA made investors feel like geniuses.

And in 2025 the same thing.

But the point is that 2023 was a bad year (thanks entirely to the regional banking crisis).

Is that kind of failure likely in the future?

No, unless rates go back to zero for a decade, in which case the Fed would have to raise rates due to another inflation spike.

But the point is that CTA isn't perfect, even if its performance since the regional banking crisis.

Bottom Line: I Trust CTA To Protect My Family Portfolio In Any Market Storm (And 51% Of My Net Worth)

Given the freak reason CTA crashed 17% in the regional banking crisis, its objective superiority in returns, and its negative downside capture ratio, I have strong confidence in it.

CTA is in the top 1% of peers in the last year, the top 16% YTD, the top 9% in the previous three months, and the top 16% in the last month.

As we've seen, it's No. 1 out of 66 managed futures funds since inception (and #1 among all hedging strategies).

  • On March 8, CTA will achieve a three-year track record.
  • And become a 99th percentile (5-star rated) ETF in Morningstar.

Management is very transparent about how CTA is managed and releases the complete portfolio daily, so I have strong confidence in what I own when I own CTA.

  • CTA Fund Deep Dive Live (CIO explaining in detail how it works).
  • 100% rules-based strategy.
  • Making it replicable over time (not "management getting lucky")

My portfolio is 52% commodities, 46% stocks, and poised for a potentially record year.

Fundamentals for The 44% Of My Portfolio That Are Stocks

Morningstar's weighted consensus (all its analysts) estimate for the fundamentally justified total return potential for the next 12 months is 24%.

That works out to a $1.5 million portfolio profit if and only if each of my companies grows as analysts expect, returns to its historical fair value, and CTA delivers its historical 14% CAGR returns.

That's not a forecast; it's just a reasonable estimate of what my portfolio's combination of 44% stocks, 52% commodities, and 4% bonds (short bonds) can deliver.

  • My 2025 charitable donations are locked in at $106K ($165K base case, $275K best case).

Most people don't want to own as much CTA as I do. That's understandable.

Mine is an AI-powered optimized portfolio that doesn't have to answer anyone other than my risk management rules.

  • I don't have to worry about "market envy."
  • I won't get fired for not matching market returns in any given year.

Given my understanding of CTA's mechanics and my trust in management's transparency and skill (confirmed by three years of peer-crushing returns), I sleep well at night with my 56% position in CTA, which represents almost $3 million.

How much conviction do I have that CTA will remain the best trade war hedge? Or the best hedge of any kind for any future correction? Well, enough to invest 51% of my net worth into it.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Alibaba Bets $52B On AI, Cloud
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Alibaba (BABA) to invest over $52 billion in AI and cloud infrastructure over next three years. (00:24) Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY) to acquire Just Eat Takeaway.com (OTCPK:JTKWY) for €4.1B in all-cash deal. (01:41) Shein's 2024 profit dropped nearly 40%: report. (02:35)

This is an abridged transcript.

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) on Monday unveiled plans to invest at least 380 billion yuan ($52.44 billion) in its cloud computing and artificial intelligence infrastructure over the next three years.

The investment, which exceeds Alibaba’s total AI and cloud spending over the past decade, comes as global competition between the United States, China, and Europe heats up.

The e-commerce giant had said it had plans to invest in the sector while announcing its results. During its latest earnings call, Alibaba CEO Eddie Wu emphasized that cloud computing remains Alibaba’s clearest revenue driver in AI, with demand for AI hosting services surging.

Alibaba‘s Cloud Intelligence Group’s revenue—excluding revenue from Alibaba-consolidated subsidiaries—grew 11% year-over-year for the three months ended December 31.

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) has been developing large language models, or LLMs, to compete with its Western and local rivals. Last month, the company unveiled its Qwen2.5-VL model, which it said fared better than Microsoft (MSFT)-backed OpenAI’s GPT-4o, Amazon (AMZN)-backed Anthropic’s Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Google’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Gemini 2.0 Flash in math, document analysis, video analysis and question-answering evaluations.

Prosus (OTCPK:PROSY) (OTCPK:PROSF) announces plans to acquire Just Eat Takeaway's (OTCPK:TKAYF) (OTCPK:JTKWY) entire issued share capital for €20.30 per share via an all-cash public offer on the Amsterdam exchange for €4.1 billion ($4.3 billion).

The all-cash offer represents a 49% premium to the 3-month VWAP and a 22% premium to its highest share price in three months. The stock closed at €12.43 on Friday.

Just Eat Takeaway is a food delivery platform in 17 international markets. It generated €26.3 billion in GTV in 2024 and an adjusted EBITDA of €460 million.

The deal is subject to customary pre-offer and offer conditions, including obtaining regulatory approvals.

Shein’s profit reportedly dropped by more than a third last year, ahead of the company’s planned initial public offering in London.

The Financial Times reported Sunday, citing two people familiar with the matter that Shein’s 2024 profit sank nearly 40% to $1B, pressured by a poor final three months and competition from Temu (PDD), the sources said.

According to the report, the people said sales rose 19% to $38B, and one said the numbers came from internal projections ahead of final results.

Bloomberg reported last week that Shein had been under pressure to cut its valuation to around $30B ahead of the IPO in the UK.

According to Bloomberg, shareholders suggested that the change was needed to get the IPO across the finish line.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Bybit hack: $1.5B stolen from ethereum wallet in largest ever crypto heist

Elon Musk’s DOGE laying off workers at the auto safety agency overseeing Tesla - report

'Captain America: Brave New World' crashes in second weekend

Catalyst watch:

  • The post-IPO quiet period expires on Smithfield Foods (SFD) and Beta Bionics (BBNX) to free up analysts to post ratings.

  • The tender offer for QXO's hostile takeover bid of Beacon Roofing (BECN) will expire.

  • Applied Materials (AMAT) management will participate in a panel discussion during the SPIE 2025 conference.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is flat at $70/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.5% at $95,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is up 0.2% and the DAX is up 0.6%. The market in Japan was closed due to a holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket:Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) shares gained 3% after the company announced plans to acquire all outstanding common shares of Calibre Mining (OTCQX:CXBMF) in a court-approved deal.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Chicago Fed National Activity Index

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Greenback Trims Initial Losses: Reversal In The Making?
data.description:
Overview

With Japanese markets closed for a national holiday, the dollar was offered in early turnover today, and its recent losses were initially extended against several currency pairs. However, it recovered, and early losses were pared or reversed. Among the G10 currencies, the dollar is higher against the Swiss franc and Japanese yen. The German election results were very much in line with the polls and a CDU/CSU-SPD coalition seems likely, and maybe the Greens too. Emerging market currencies are mostly firmer against the US dollar.

After the pre-weekend tumble in the US equities, Asia Pacific markets mostly fell today. Of the large bourses, Australia was an exception with its small gain. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is up about 0.25%. US index futures are pointing to a higher open. Benchmark 10-year yields are little changed, putting the US 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) near 4.43%. Gold is firm near $2940, a little below last week's record. April WTI opened below $70 and has recovered to about $70.65 in European turnover.

USD: The Dollar Index made a new low since early December in Asia Pacific trading today near 106.10 before recovering fully by late European morning turnover. A close above last Friday's high 106.75 would lift the technical tone. A move above last week's high of almost 107.40 and a move above would suggest a low may be in place. The economic diary features a couple of Fed surveys and house prices today and tomorrow. Around nine Fed officials are scheduled to speak this week, beginning with Logan, Barr and Barkin tomorrow. The views are fairly well known at this juncture.

Euro: In the rally in Asia Pacific today, the euro made a marginal new high for the month but was turned back before taking out the January high near $1.0535. It has turned toward session low, a little above $1.0460. A break of $1.0450 set up a test on last week's low near $1.04. That may be the neckline of a double top pattern, whose measuring objective would be around $1.03. Initially, a break of $1.04 may find support near $1.0365. Earlier, the January CPI was confirmed at 2.5% (and 2.7% core). The German election results were in line with the polls. The CDU won a plurality of votes, but given the fragmented nature of German politics, a coalition government will be necessary and the two parties in the current government, SPD and Greens, are ironically the most likely to help form it. It is easy to be cynical and expect a continuation of the status quo, more or less. Instead, it is possible that the election makes possible new initiatives to boost integration and strengthen local defense capabilities. The fact that the German IFO survey was flat (85.2) as a result of the decline in the current assessment and a rise in expectations.

CNY: For more than three months, the dollar has been trading in a roughly CNH7.21-CNH7.3750 trading range on the broad and mostly CNH7.25-CNY7.30 on the narrow. It fell to about CNH7.2260 today before rebounding back above CNH7.25. Officials seems content. Some pressure on the yuan appears to have moderated by the narrowing of the 10-year yield discount to the US and the better performing equity market, especially for the mainland companies listed in HK. The PBOC continues to set the dollar's reference rate in a narrow range that has moved more than 0.01%-0.02%, with two exceptions this year and today is one of them: the fix was set at CNY7.1717, 0.03% above Friday's.

JPY: The dollar had been trying to re-establish a foothold above JPY150 before the weekend, and before the unexpected contraction in the US services PMI and deterioration in US consumer confidence saw the US 10-year yield extend its decline for the third consecutive session to 4.40%, almost 17 basis points lower than the mid-week high. While Tokyo markets were closed today for the Emperor's Birthday, the dollar was sold to a marginal new low, slightly below JPY148.90. Taking out JPY149.20 means that the dollar has retraced half of its mid-Sept to Jan 10 rally. The December low was near JPY148.65 and the next retracement (61.8%) is around JPY147. It has recovered to new session highs in Europe near JPY149.65. A close above JPY150 may help stabilize the technical tone.

GBP: Sterling stalled ahead of the weekend after reaching a new two-month high near $1.2680. The profit-taking by intraday players saw it pullback by a little more than half a cent. It made a marginal new high today, slightly above $1.2690. It was repulsed in front of $1.2700 and fell to session lows near $1.2625 before finding support. Last week's low was closer to $1.2565, and the band of support may extend toward $1.2550.

CAD: Last week, the US dollar held above the two-month low set February 14 near CAD1.4150. The upside was limited to almost CAD1.4250, holding in front CAD1.4260, which was previously support and now resistance. The greenback finished last week near new session highs around CAD1.4230 and has held below there today. The Canadian dollar seems particularly vulnerable to the tariffs that the US threatens starting early next month. The Canadian dollar is firmer than when it settled last year (~CAD1.4390), while the risks emanating from the US would seem to have risen sharply.

AUD: Before the weekend, the Australian dollar reached a new two-month high and came within five-hundredths of a cent of meeting the (38.2%) retracement of losses since last September's high ($0.6940). The Aussie was turned back after reaching almost $0.6410. It recorded session lows in the North American session near $0.6355. It has held below $0.6400 today and found support near $06360. It can fall back to $0.6330 without inflicting much technical damage.

MXN: The dollar recorded a new low for the month near MXN20.20 in the middle of last week. It recovered but only to stall near the 20-day moving average, a little below MXN20.48. Today's high has been slightly above MXN20.43. The peso, like the Canadian dollar, seems vulnerable. The 25% tariff (10% for Canadian energy) ostensibly for undocumented migration and fentanyl that were postponed are back in play as soon as March 4. Mexico reports inflation for the first half of February today. The headline rate is seen ticking up to 3.7% from about 3.5% and the core rate steady near 3.6%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
